narrative:
Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors, estimated a 48% chance that you would fail the exam. This threshold triggered the rejection to help the university manage its bar passage rates and support its students' long-term success. As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, but the model focused heavily on key academic indicators from your record.

The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year—far below the typical student's midpoint around the 5th or 6th decile. This low performance signal stood out as the strongest driver, suggesting to the model a potential struggle with the sustained rigor needed for bar preparation. Next in importance was your LSAT score of 36, slightly under the average of 36.7, which worked in your favor by nudging the prediction toward passing, as it showed solid reasoning ability compared to many applicants.

Your undergraduate GPA of 2.6, below the dataset average of 3.21, also pulled somewhat toward failure but less forcefully than your later school grades. Overall, while your LSAT and GPA offered some positive counterbalance, they couldn't offset the dominant impact of that low Year 3 decile in the model's eyes. Compared to typical students who pass the bar on their first try, your trajectory dipped more sharply in later undergrad years, which is a common red flag the model weighs heavily.

This isn't a judgment on your potential or the barriers you may have faced as a Black man navigating these systems—many strong candidates share similar backgrounds and succeed with targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through a post-baccalaureate option, and aim to retake the LSAT for a higher score. Building a stronger narrative around your resilience, like through internships or bar prep courses, could also help in reapplying or appealing to schools with more holistic reviews. With focused improvements, you can shift these predictions in your favor next time.
